md: We've met three classical centers of a triangle: the **circumcenter** $O$ (perpendicular bisectors), the **centroid** $G$ (medians), the **orthocenter** $H$ (altitudes). Together with the **incenter** $I$, these are four distinct named centers of a triangle.

Here is a stunning fact, discovered by Leonhard Euler in 1765: in any non-equilateral triangle, **the centers $O$, $G$, and $H$ are collinear** â€” they all lie on a single line. That line is called the **Euler line** of the triangle.
More: the centroid $G$ sits **exactly one-third of the way from $O$ to $H$**. So $OG : GH = 1 : 2$ â€” every time, for every triangle.

blocks.title: The Euler relation
blocks.body:
$\boxed{\;\vec{OH} = 3\,\vec{OG}\;}$

Equivalently: $H = 3G - 2O$, or $G = O + \tfrac{1}{3}(H - O)$.

Drag any vertex in the widget above. The three colored centers move (red $O$, green $G$, purple $H$), but they stay on the dashed cyan **Euler line**, and the $1:2$ ratio is preserved.

md: ### Why the line exists â€” proof sketch
Use the centroid formula $G = (A + B + C)/3$ and the *orthocenter formula* (which can be derived) $H = A + B + C - 2O$. Then:

So $H - O$ is exactly $3$ times $G - O$. This means $O$, $G$, $H$ are collinear (all along the direction $G - O$), and the distances satisfy $|OH| = 3 |OG|$ â€” i.e., $G$ is one-third of the way from $O$ to $H$, leaving $GH$ as two-thirds. So $OG : GH = 1 : 2$. âˆŽ

**Equilateral triangle.** All four centers coincide at the same point. The Euler 'line' is just a point â€” degenerate. (Or you can say it's *every* line through that point, depending on your aesthetics.)
**No other case.** In any non-equilateral triangle, $O$, $G$, $H$ are distinct collinear points, and the Euler line is a genuine line.

blocks.title: Who else lives on the Euler line?
blocks.body:
It's getting crowded.

**The nine-point center** $N$ â€” the center of the **nine-point circle**, which passes through nine special points: three midpoints of sides, three feet of altitudes, three midpoints of segments from each vertex to $H$. The nine-point center sits at the midpoint of $OH$.

So on the Euler line, in order: $O$, then $N$ (midpoint of $OH$), then $G$ (one-third from $O$), then $H$. With ratios:

$O : N : G : H = 0 : 3 : 2 : 6$

(measuring distances from $O$ along the line).

*Not* on the Euler line in general: the **incenter** $I$. The incenter sits on the Euler line only when the triangle is **isosceles**.

md: **Right triangle â‡’ circumcenter is midpoint of hypotenuse $BC$.** $O = (3, 4)$.
**Right triangle â‡’ orthocenter at the right-angle vertex.** $H = A = (0, 0)$.
All three centers lie on the line through $O(3, 4)$ in direction $(-3, -4)$ â€” i.e., the line from $(3, 4)$ to $(0, 0)$. That's the Euler line of this triangle.

blocks.title: Why this is one of math's most quietly beautiful theorems
blocks.body: Three completely different constructions â€” perpendicular bisectors, medians, altitudes â€” give three completely different points. Generically you'd expect three random points to form a triangle. But they don't. They lie on a line, with a fixed ratio. This kind of unexpected structure is what makes geometry rewarding.
